content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 2 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in 1 Corinthians Chapter 2 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 2:1 -- "proclaiming/telling" and "lofty speech/impressive wisdom" and "mystery"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I...came not according to excellence of speech or of wisdom, announcing to you the mystery of God" | kath' hyperoken as "according to excellence"; katangellon as "announcing" |
| LOGOS | "did not come proclaiming to you the mystery of God with lofty speech or wisdom" | kath' hyperoken as "lofty"; katangellon as "proclaiming" |
| PNEUMA | "I didn't use lofty words and impressive wisdom to tell you God's mystery" | Adds "impressive"; simplifies verb to "tell"; contracts negation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Proclaiming" better captures the public, authoritative nature of katangello than PNEUMA's "tell." "Lofty speech or wisdom" renders the Greek construction clearly. PNEUMA's addition of "impressive" is an interpretive expansion.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:2 -- "decided/determined" and structural differences

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For I determined not to know anything among you except Yeshua Christos, and this [one] crucified" | "determined"; demonstrative "this [one]" preserved |
| LOGOS | "For I decided to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and him crucified" | "decided"; "him crucified" |
| PNEUMA | "For I decided that while I was with you I would forget everything except Jesus Christ, the one who was crucified" | Adds "while I was with you"; "forget" for "not know"; "the one who was crucified" as relative clause |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Decided" and "determined" are both acceptable for ekrino. PNEUMA's "forget everything" misrepresents the Greek -- Paul is not saying he forgot other knowledge but that he resolved to center exclusively on Christ crucified. The relative clause restructuring weakens the abrupt force of "him crucified."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "forget" flagged as inaccurate.

---

## 2:3 -- "weakness and in fear and much trembling" vs. "timid and trembling"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I was with you in weakness and in fear and in much trembling" | Full triadic phrase preserved |
| LOGOS | "I was with you in weakness and in fear and much trembling" | Nearly identical to EMET |
| PNEUMA | "I came to you in weakness -- timid and trembling" | Reduces triad to two elements; changes "fear" to "timid"; drops "much" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The three-part phrase (weakness, fear, trembling) is a deliberate rhetorical construction that PNEUMA reduces. "Fear" (phobos) should not be softened to "timid," which implies a personality trait rather than the existential dread Paul describes.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:4 -- "plausible words of wisdom" vs. "clever and persuasive speeches" and "demonstration of the Spirit"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not in persuasive words of wisdom but in demonstration of [the] Spirit and of power" | peithois as "persuasive"; apodeixis as "demonstration" |
| LOGOS | "not in plausible words of wisdom, but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power" | peithois as "plausible" |
| PNEUMA | "Rather than using clever and persuasive speeches, I relied only on the power of the Holy Spirit" | Expands "clever and persuasive"; collapses "Spirit and power" into "power of the Holy Spirit"; adds "only" and "relied" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Plausible" for peithois captures the rhetorical nuance (words designed to seem convincing). PNEUMA's collapsing of "Spirit and power" into "power of the Holy Spirit" loses the distinction Paul draws -- the Spirit's demonstration and divine power are presented as two coordinated elements. PNEUMA also adds "only" and "relied," neither present in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:6 -- "mature/mature believers" and "doomed to pass away/soon forgotten"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "wisdom we speak among the mature...the [ones] being brought to nothing" | teleioi as "mature"; katargeo as "being brought to nothing" |
| LOGOS | "Yet among the mature we do impart wisdom...who are doomed to pass away" | teleioi as "mature"; katargeo as "doomed to pass away" |
| PNEUMA | "when I am among mature believers, I do speak with words of wisdom...who are soon forgotten" | Adds "believers" to teleioi; katargeo as "soon forgotten"; adds "I" as subject |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Doomed to pass away" effectively captures the eschatological force of katargeo (being rendered ineffective/abolished). PNEUMA's "soon forgotten" trivializes the concept -- these rulers are not merely forgotten but actively abolished by God's purposes. Adding "believers" to "mature" is an unnecessary restriction.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:7 -- "secret and hidden wisdom" vs. "mystery of God -- his plan that was previously hidden"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "we speak [the] wisdom of God in [a] mystery, the [one] having been hidden, which God predetermined before the ages for the glory of us" | en mysterio as "in a mystery"; proorizo as "predetermined" |
| LOGOS | "we impart a secret and hidden wisdom of God, which God decreed before the ages for our glory" | Combines mysterion + apokekrymmenen into "secret and hidden"; proorizo as "decreed" |
| PNEUMA | "the wisdom I speak of is the mystery of God -- his plan that was previously hidden, even though he made it for our ultimate glory before the world began" | Adds "his plan"; "ultimate glory"; "before the world began" for "before the ages" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Secret and hidden wisdom of God" effectively combines the two Greek concepts. "Decreed" for proorizo is appropriate. PNEUMA's additions ("his plan," "ultimate," "before the world began") expand beyond the Greek text. EMET's "predetermined" is lexically precise but carries heavy theological baggage in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:8 -- "Lord of glory / Lord of the glory / glorious Lord"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they would not have crucified the Lord of the glory" | Preserves article: ton kyrion tes doxes |
| LOGOS | "they would not have crucified the Lord of glory" | Standard English form without article |
| PNEUMA | "they would not have crucified our glorious Lord" | Converts genitive to adjective; adds "our" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering ("the Lord of glory"). This is a fixed christological title. PNEUMA's "glorious Lord" (adjective) diminishes the title -- "Lord of glory" means the Lord who possesses/embodies divine glory, not merely a lord who is glorious. The genitive is possessive/qualitative, not merely descriptive.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's adjectival rendering flagged as a theological weakening.

---

## 2:9 -- "heart of man imagined / upon the heart of man did not arise / no mind has imagined"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "upon [the] heart of man did not arise, what God prepared for the [ones] loving him" | Literal rendering of epi kardian anthropou ouk anebe |
| LOGOS | "nor the heart of man imagined, what God has prepared for those who love him" | "imagined" interprets anebe (ascended) applied to the heart |
| PNEUMA | "and no mind has imagined what God has prepared for those who love him" | Changes "heart" to "mind"; "imagined" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Heart" should be preserved (kardia is the Greek term), not replaced with "mind" (PNEUMA), since the biblical concept of "heart" includes thought, will, and imagination. "Imagined" effectively captures the metaphorical use of anabaino ("ascend") with "heart" as its domain.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's substitution of "mind" for "heart" rejected.

---

## 2:10 -- "deep things/depths/deep secrets"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Spirit searches all [things], even the deep [things] of God" | ta bathe as "the deep things" |
| LOGOS | "the Spirit searches everything, even the depths of God" | ta bathe as "the depths" |
| PNEUMA | "his Spirit searches out everything and shows us God's deep secrets" | Adds "shows us"; ta bathe as "deep secrets" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Depths of God" is a powerful metaphor that should not be reduced to "deep secrets" (PNEUMA), which implies specific hidden information rather than the unfathomable profundity of God's being. PNEUMA's addition of "shows us" is not in this verse.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:11 -- "comprehends/knows" for ginosko and oida

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who among men knows the [things] of the man except the spirit of the man...the [things] of God no one knows except the Spirit of God" | Both oida and ginosko as "knows" |
| LOGOS | "who knows a person's thoughts except the spirit of that person...no one comprehends the thoughts of God except the Spirit of God" | oida as "knows"; ginosko as "comprehends"; ta tou anthropou interpreted as "thoughts" |
| PNEUMA | "No one can know a person's thoughts except that person's own spirit, and no one can know God's thoughts except God's own Spirit" | Both as "know"; adds "can"; adds "own" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The distinction between "knows" and "comprehends" effectively captures the slight difference between oida (intuitive knowing) and ginosko (comprehending, grasping). PNEUMA's rendering is clear but loses this nuance and adds "can" and "own" not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:13 -- "interpreting spiritual truths to those who are spiritual" vs. "using the Spirit's words to explain the Spirit's truths"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "interpreting spiritual [things] to spiritual [persons]" | pneumatikois pneumatika -- preserves the ambiguity of the dative |
| LOGOS | "interpreting spiritual truths to those who are spiritual" | Takes dative as personal (masculine) |
| PNEUMA | "we speak words given to us by the Spirit, using the Spirit's words to explain the Spirit's truths" | Major expansion; resolves ambiguity by tripling "Spirit's"; adds "given to us" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering, which takes pneumatikois as masculine (dative of persons: "to those who are spiritual"). This interpretation fits the context (vv.14-15 distinguish spiritual from natural persons). However, EMET's note that the dative could be neuter ("by spiritual means") is acknowledged -- the ambiguity may be intentional. PNEUMA's expansion adds material not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's ambiguity note preserved.

---

## 2:14 -- "natural person / natural man / people who aren't spiritual"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the natural man does not receive the [things] of the Spirit of God, for they are foolishness to him" | psychikos as "natural"; dechomai as "receive" |
| LOGOS | "The natural person does not accept the things of the Spirit of God, for they are folly to him" | psychikos as "natural person"; dechomai as "accept" |
| PNEUMA | "But people who aren't spiritual can't receive these truths from God's Spirit. It all sounds foolish to them" | Defines by negation ("aren't spiritual"); "can't receive" vs. "does not accept"; "sounds foolish" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Natural person" (gender-neutral) for psychikos anthropos is appropriate. "Accept" for dechomai better conveys the volitional aspect (choosing not to welcome) than PNEUMA's "can't receive" (which suggests inability rather than unwillingness, though v.14b does address inability). PNEUMA's "people who aren't spiritual" loses the technical term psychikos.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:15 -- "judges/discerns/evaluates"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the spiritual [one] discerns all [things], but he himself is discerned by no one" | anakrino as "discerns" (both occurrences) |
| LOGOS | "The spiritual person judges all things, but is himself to be judged by no one" | anakrino as "judges" |
| PNEUMA | "Those who are spiritual can evaluate all things, but they themselves cannot be evaluated by others" | anakrino as "evaluate"; plural rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Judges" for anakrino captures the forensic sense (examine, investigate, assess). PNEUMA's "evaluate" is acceptable but weaker. EMET's "discerns" emphasizes perception over assessment. The singular "the spiritual person" (LOGOS) better matches the Greek singular.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:16 -- "understood/knew/can know" and "instruct/teach"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For who knew the mind of [the] Lord? Who will instruct him?" But we have the mind of Christos" | egnoo as "knew"; symbibazo as "instruct" |
| LOGOS | "For who has understood the mind of the Lord so as to instruct him?" But we have the mind of Christ" | egnoo as "has understood"; combines the two questions |
| PNEUMA | "Who can know the Lord's thoughts? Who knows enough to teach him?" But we understand these things, for we have the mind of Christ" | "can know" adds modality; "teach" for symbibazo; adds "we understand these things" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Has understood" for egnoo captures the perfective aspect. LOGOS's combining of the citation into a single question with purpose clause ("so as to instruct") follows standard translation of the Isaiah 40:13 LXX citation. PNEUMA adds "we understand these things" which is not in the Greek -- Paul's claim is simply "we have the mind of Christ."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA adds interpretive material not in Greek | vv.1, 2, 4, 7, 8, 10, 13, 16 | "impressive," "forget," "only," "his plan," "our," "shows us," "given to us" |
| PNEUMA weakens theological terms through paraphrase | vv.6, 8, 9, 10, 14 | "soon forgotten" for katargeo, "glorious Lord" for "Lord of glory," "mind" for "heart" |
| EMET preserves Greek syntax at cost of English clarity | vv.1, 7, 9, 11 | Bracket-heavy renderings, article markers |
| LOGOS-SOPHIA alignment | Nearly all verses | LOGOS provided the base for consensus |
| Verbal nuance in translation of key terms | vv.11, 14, 15 | ginosko vs. oida, dechomai, anakrino |
| Handling of ambiguous Greek constructions | v.13 | pneumatikois pneumatika (masculine vs. neuter dative) |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Chapter 2 continues the pattern from Chapter 1: LOGOS and SOPHIA's consensus align closely, EMET provides valuable lexical and syntactic precision, and PNEUMA's dynamic approach frequently introduces material not present in the Greek. The most theologically significant divergences concern the christological title "Lord of glory" (v.8), where PNEUMA weakens it to an adjective; the biblical anthropology of "heart" vs. "mind" (v.9); and the technical term psychikos (v.14). PNEUMA's rendering of v.2 ("forget everything") was flagged as inaccurate. The chapter demonstrates that dynamic translation carries particular risks in densely theological passages where precision of terminology matters.

---
